questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is given an eye drop during an ophthalmology exam that stimulates cholinergic (parasympathetic) activity in the iris. Which of the following effects is most consistent with cholinergic activation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constriction of the iris producing pupillary miosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilation of the bronchial smooth muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: A decrease in gastric acid secretion

explanation.summary: Cholinergic (parasympathetic) activation drives 'rest and digest' effects, including pupillary constriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that cholinergic activation causes the iris to contract, producing pin-point (constricted) pupils. Parasympathetic tone promotes the rest-and-digest functions of the body.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cholinergic activation decreases heart rate through M2 receptors, rather than increasing it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cholinergic activation increases bronchial contraction, not bronchodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cholinergic activation increases gastric secretions and acid production as part of digestion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Skeletal-muscle vasodilation is not the cholinergic effect described; parasympathetic tone supports digestion and elimination.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A researcher studies a receptor that, when activated, triggers phospholipase C-beta to generate IP3 and DAG and raises intracellular calcium. Which of the following best describes this Gq-GPCR pathway?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of the adenylyl cyclase enzyme

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activation of IP3 and calcium release

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of cAMP production by adenylyl cyclase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of cAMP through protein kinase A

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct opening of a ligand-gated sodium channel

explanation.summary: The Gq pathway signals through IP3/DAG and calcium rather than through cAMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick teaches that the Gq pathway differs from Gs and Gi because it involves calcium release and signaling via calcium-dependent pathways, activating IP3 and increasing cytosolic calcium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Inhibition of adenylyl cyclase occurs with the Gi pathway, not Gq.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Activation of cAMP by adenylyl cyclase is the Gs (stimulatory) pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting cAMP via protein kinase A describes the Gi pathway, not the calcium-based Gq pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gq is a G-protein-coupled receptor pathway, not a ligand-gated ion channel like the nicotinic receptor.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man in septic shock is started on norepinephrine to raise his blood pressure. At therapeutic doses, norepinephrine has the greatest affinity for which of the following receptors?

explanation.summary: Norepinephrine acts primarily on alpha-1 receptors to raise blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states norepinephrine has more alpha-1 effects than alpha-2 or beta receptors (alpha-1 greater than alpha-2), causing constriction of vascular beds and increasing systemic vascular resistance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-2 receptors are less affected than alpha-1 at therapeutic doses.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-1 receptors are only minimally affected by norepinephrine at therapeutic doses.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-2 receptors are minimally affected; norepinephrine is not selective for beta receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Norepinephrine is an adrenergic agonist and does not act on muscarinic cholinergic receptors.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with decompensated heart failure and poor cardiac contractility is given dobutamine to improve her cardiac output. Dobutamine produces its positive inotropic effect mainly by stimulating which receptor?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is a positive inotrope acting through beta-1 receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states dobutamine has high affinity for beta-1 receptors, and this beta-1 effect increases cardiac contractility (positive inotropic effect) as well as heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-2 activity is mostly in the lungs and mediates bronchodilation, not the inotropic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dobutamine does not have much alpha-2 effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-3 receptors are mostly in the urinary bladder and do not mediate inotropy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dopaminergic D1 receptors are targeted by dopamine in the renal vasculature, not by dobutamine.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Which of the following neurotransmitters is released by cholinergic neurons to mediate transmission?

explanation.summary: Cholinergic neurons release acetylcholine.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines cholinergic neurons as those that release acetylcholine (ACh) to mediate transmission at the autonomic ganglia and at parasympathetic effector organs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine is released by adrenergic neurons, not cholinergic neurons.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Epinephrine is released by the adrenal medulla into the blood, an adrenergic signal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dobutamine is a synthetic drug, not an endogenous neurotransmitter.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dopamine is not the neurotransmitter of cholinergic transmission described here.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: During a physiology demonstration, activation of a specific adrenergic receptor in the heart increases the heart rate. Which receptor is most responsible for this effect?

explanation.summary: Beta-1 receptors increase cardiac muscle contraction and heart rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states adrenergic beta-1 receptors are Gs-type GPCRs that increase cardiac muscle contraction and increase heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-1 receptors are mostly in the periphery and drive smooth-muscle (vascular) contraction, not heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-2 receptors mediate smooth-muscle relaxation and do not increase heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Muscarinic M2 receptors decrease heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alpha-2 receptors are Gi-coupled and are not responsible for increasing heart rate.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman develops anaphylaxis with wheezing and hypotension after a bee sting, and epinephrine is administered. Which receptor mediates the bronchodilation that helps relieve her wheezing?

explanation.summary: Epinephrine causes bronchodilation through beta-2 receptor activation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick teaches that epinephrine has equal affinity for alpha and beta receptors and is helpful in bronchodilation through beta-2 activation in the lungs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-1 activation causes vasoconstriction and raises blood pressure, not bronchodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-1 activation increases cardiac contractility and heart rate rather than dilating bronchi.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Muscarinic M3 activation causes bronchial constriction, the opposite effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dopaminergic D2 receptors act in the renal vasculature and do not mediate bronchodilation.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A critically ill patient is started on dopamine. In addition to its cardiac effects, dopamine activates receptors in the renal vascular beds that produce local vasodilation. Which receptors mediate this renal effect?

explanation.summary: Dopamine acts on D1 and D2 dopaminergic receptors in the renal vasculature to cause vasodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states dopamine activates D1 and D2 dopaminergic receptors, which are in the renal vascular beds and produce vasodilation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-1 activation (seen at higher dopamine doses) causes vasoconstriction, not the renal vasodilation described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-1 activation increases cardiac contractility rather than dilating renal vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Muscarinic M2 receptors act on the heart to slow rate and are not the renal dopaminergic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nicotinic nN receptors are ligand-gated channels at ganglia, not the renal vasodilatory receptors.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with cardiogenic shock has poor cardiac contractility but is not profoundly vasodilated. The team wants an agent classified primarily as an inotrope to increase contractility rather than one used primarily as a vasopressor. Which agent best fits this description?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is classified as an inotrope, whereas epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine are typically classified as vasopressors.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states dobutamine increases contractility (positive inotropic agent) and is therefore classified as an inotrope, in contrast to the agents grouped as vasopressors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine is classified as a vasopressor with potent effects on the vasculature.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Epinephrine is typically classified as a vasopressor for its potent vascular effects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dopamine is typically classified as a vasopressor in this brick.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine is a pure vasoconstrictor (pressor) and is not the inotrope described.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: All beta-adrenergic receptors couple to the same class of G protein. Activation of a beta receptor therefore leads to which of the following intracellular changes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased cAMP through Gs-mediated adenylyl cyclase activation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased cAMP through Gi-mediated inhibition of adenylyl cyclase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased IP3 and DAG through Gq-mediated phospholipase C

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct sodium influx through a ligand-gated ion channel

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased intracellular calcium through Gq inhibition

explanation.summary: Beta receptors are Gs-coupled and raise cAMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick teaches that all beta receptors are Gs-type GPCRs, and the Gs (stimulatory) pathway activates adenylyl cyclase to increase cAMP.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased cAMP via Gi describes alpha-2 and M2 receptors, not beta receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased IP3/DAG via Gq describes alpha-1 and M3 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct ion flux describes nicotinic ligand-gated channels, not GPCR-coupled beta receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gq signaling raises calcium; beta receptors do not act through Gq to lower calcium.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A student is asked how nicotinic cholinergic receptors differ from the muscarinic and adrenergic receptors of the autonomic nervous system. Which of the following statements about nicotinic receptors is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are ligand-gated ion channels activated by acetylcholine

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are Gs-coupled GPCRs that raise cAMP

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are Gq-coupled GPCRs that raise intracellular calcium

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are activated primarily by norepinephrine

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are Gi-coupled GPCRs that lower cAMP

explanation.summary: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels opened by acetylcholine.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick highlights that nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated channels activated by ACh, distinguishing them from the GPCR-based muscarinic and adrenergic receptors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Gs-coupled cAMP signaling describes beta-adrenergic receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gq-coupled calcium signaling describes alpha-1 and M3 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nicotinic receptors are cholinergic and respond to acetylcholine, not norepinephrine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gi-coupled cAMP inhibition describes alpha-2 and M2 receptors, not nicotinic channels.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A patient in shock is given a vasopressor to restore blood pressure. Which mechanism best explains how a vasopressor raises blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased vasoconstriction that raises systemic vascular resistance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced cardiac contractility that lowers cardiac output

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slowing of the heart rate through M2 receptor activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased bronchial secretions through cholinergic activation

explanation.summary: Vasopressors raise blood pressure by increasing vasoconstriction and systemic vascular resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines vasopressors as agents that increase constriction of blood vessels; the resulting rise in vascular resistance increases blood pressure, which is useful in acute, hemodynamically unstable patients.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vasodilation lowers vascular resistance and blood pressure, the opposite of a vasopressor effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reducing contractility lowers cardiac output and would not raise blood pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Slowing the heart rate is a cholinergic (M2) effect and does not describe vasopressor action.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased bronchial secretions is a cholinergic effect unrelated to raising blood pressure.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension is started on clonidine. Which of the following best explains how this drug lowers his blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It blocks alpha-1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases norepinephrine release from sympathetic nerve terminals

choices.label: E
choices.text: It raises cAMP in cardiac myocytes to slow the heart

explanation.summary: Clonidine is a central alpha-2 agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alpha-2 receptors sit on the terminals of presynaptic sympathetic neurons in the brain. When clonidine activates them, norepinephrine release is decreased, reducing activation of CNS adrenergic receptors and lowering blood pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Clonidine is an alpha-2 agonist, not an alpha-1 blocker; it does not act as an antagonist on vascular smooth muscle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Clonidine decreases, not increases, norepinephrine release; increasing release would raise blood pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ACE inhibition is a separate drug class and is not the mechanism of clonidine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alpha-2 receptors are Gi-coupled and lower cAMP; clonidine does not raise cardiac cAMP.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman in the intensive care unit is given intravenous phenylephrine to treat hypotension. Shortly afterward her heart rate slows. Which of the following best explains this change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reflex tachycardia from arterial baroreceptor unloading

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct alpha-1 stimulation of the sinoatrial node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reflex bradycardia from a baroreceptor-mediated decrease in sympathetic outflow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased norepinephrine release in the brainstem

explanation.summary: Alpha-1 agonist-induced rises in systemic vascular resistance trigger reflex bradycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylephrine increases alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction and systemic vascular resistance. This activates the baroreceptor reflex, causing a decrease in sympathetic outflow and therefore a fall in heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rising blood pressure loads, rather than unloads, the baroreceptors, and the reflex slows rather than speeds the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The heart-rate change is a baroreceptor reflex, not direct alpha-1 action on the SA node.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phenylephrine is an alpha-1 agonist and does not block cardiac beta-1 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine acts peripherally on vasculature; it does not increase brainstem norepinephrine release.

questions.stem: Phenylephrine binds alpha-1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle. Which of the following intracellular signaling sequences most directly produces the resulting contraction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gs activation increases adenylyl cyclase, raising cAMP

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gi activation decreases adenylyl cyclase, lowering cAMP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gq activation stimulates phospholipase C, forming IP3 and releasing calcium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct opening of voltage-gated potassium channels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activation of guanylyl cyclase increasing cGMP

explanation.summary: Alpha-1 receptors are Gq-coupled and signal through the PLC/IP3/calcium pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: The alpha-1 receptor is a Gq-type GPCR. Activation stimulates phospholipase C, which converts PIP2 to IP3. IP3 releases calcium from the endoplasmic reticulum, raising cytosolic calcium and driving smooth muscle contraction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gs/cAMP signaling is not the alpha-1 pathway and would not drive this contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gi-mediated lowering of cAMP is the alpha-2 pathway, not the alpha-1 contraction mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Opening potassium channels would hyperpolarize and relax, not contract, the muscle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: cGMP signaling promotes vasodilation and is unrelated to alpha-1 activation.

questions.stem: A patient uses an intranasal phenylephrine spray for congestion. Which of the following best describes how the drug relieves the congestion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vasoconstriction of nasal mucosal vessels that decreases mucosal edema

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vasodilation that flushes secretions from the sinuses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blockade of histamine receptors in the nasal mucosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of mucus-secreting glands via muscarinic blockade

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stimulation of beta-2 receptors relaxing airway smooth muscle

explanation.summary: Phenylephrine decongests by alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction of nasal vessels.
explanation.whyCorrect: Direct stimulation of alpha-1 receptors on the nasal mucosa causes vasoconstriction of local nasal vessels. This decreases mucosal edema, producing the decongestant effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phenylephrine causes vasoconstriction, not vasodilation, and its effect is on vessel tone, not flushing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It is an alpha-1 agonist, not an antihistamine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: It does not work through muscarinic blockade of glands.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine is selective for alpha-1 receptors, not beta-2 receptors.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman who is 20 weeks pregnant develops hypertension that requires pharmacologic treatment. Which of the following agents is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Methyldopa is the alpha-2 agonist indicated for hypertension in pregnancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methyldopa can be used for all types of hypertension and is specifically indicated during pregnancy, where it is useful for patients who develop adverse effects or become intolerant to other commonly used medications.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phenylephrine is an alpha-1 agonist that raises blood pressure and would worsen hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Oxymetazoline is a topical alpha-1 agonist for decongestion and eye redness, not for hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Clonidine is an antihypertensive but is not the agent specifically indicated during pregnancy in this brick.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Norepinephrine is a vasopressor that raises blood pressure and would be inappropriate.

questions.stem: Methyldopa is often called a false neurotransmitter. Which of the following best explains this description?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It irreversibly blocks reuptake of norepinephrine at the synapse

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is metabolized to alpha-methylnorepinephrine, which is released in place of norepinephrine

choices.label: C
choices.text: It competitively antagonizes norepinephrine at postsynaptic alpha-1 receptors

choices.label: D
choices.text: It depletes dopamine stores in the substantia nigra

choices.label: E
choices.text: It increases synthesis of norepinephrine from tyrosine

explanation.summary: Methyldopa is converted to a selective alpha-2 agonist that substitutes for norepinephrine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methyldopa is taken up into sympathetic nerve terminals and metabolized to alpha-methylnorepinephrine, a selective alpha-2 agonist. This metabolite is loaded into presynaptic vesicles and released in place of norepinephrine, so activating presynaptic alpha-2 receptors decreases sympathetic outflow and lowers blood pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Methyldopa is not a reuptake inhibitor; it is stored and released as a false transmitter.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It acts as an alpha-2 agonist through its metabolite, not as an alpha-1 antagonist.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Its antihypertensive action involves sympathetic terminals, not dopamine depletion in the substantia nigra.
whyWrongByChoice.E: It substitutes for norepinephrine rather than increasing norepinephrine synthesis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man taking clonidine for hypertension reports a persistently dry mouth. Which of the following terms best describes this adverse effect?

explanation.summary: Dry mouth from clonidine is called xerostomia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Xerostomia, or dryness of the mouth, is a common adverse effect associated with clonidine use.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypersalivation is the opposite of the dry mouth seen with clonidine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mydriasis is pupil dilation, an effect of alpha-1 stimulation in the eye, not a clonidine dry-mouth effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reflex tachycardia is not the described symptom and is not a listed clonidine effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Extravasation refers to a drug leaking into tissue at an IV site, not to dry mouth.

questions.stem: In addition to treating hypertension, clonidine is used as an adjunct for which of the following conditions?

explanation.summary: Clonidine's reduction of central norepinephrine makes it useful as an ADHD adjunct.
explanation.whyCorrect: By decreasing norepinephrine release in the brain, clonidine can help control unwanted behaviors in psychiatric conditions such as ADHD and Tourette syndrome, and it is used as an adjunct treatment for ADHD.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Circulatory shock is treated with alpha-1 vasopressors like phenylephrine, not clonidine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nasal congestion is treated with alpha-1 agonists such as phenylephrine or oxymetazoline.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pupil dilation is produced by alpha-1 agonists acting on the iris, not by clonidine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Facial erythema is treated with topical oxymetazoline, not clonidine.

questions.stem: Compared with norepinephrine and epinephrine, phenylephrine has a longer duration of action. Which of the following best explains this difference?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is not a catecholamine and therefore is not metabolized by COMT

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is highly protein bound in plasma

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is rapidly recycled by neuronal reuptake

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is a selective alpha-2 agonist that resists enzymatic breakdown

explanation.summary: Phenylephrine escapes COMT metabolism because it is not a catecholamine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike norepinephrine and epinephrine, phenylephrine is not a catecholamine, so it is not metabolized by catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT). This gives it a longer duration of action.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Its longer action is due to slower metabolism, not irreversible receptor binding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick attributes the longer duration to lack of COMT metabolism, not protein binding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Escape from COMT metabolism, not neuronal recycling, explains the longer effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine is a selective alpha-1, not alpha-2, agonist.

questions.stem: During an infusion of intravenous phenylephrine, the drug leaks out of the vein into the surrounding tissue. Which of the following complications is most likely?

explanation.summary: Extravasated phenylephrine causes intense local vasoconstriction and tissue necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Peripheral extravasation of phenylephrine into surrounding tissue leads to excessive vasoconstriction, which can cause skin necrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phenylephrine constricts vessels; extravasation causes ischemia, not vasodilation and warmth.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Its alpha-1 action raises pressure and constricts local vessels rather than causing local hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Extravasation causes a local tissue injury; the systemic reflex from phenylephrine is bradycardia, not tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Xerostomia is a clonidine effect, not a consequence of phenylephrine extravasation.

questions.stem: An ophthalmologist applies phenylephrine drops before a retinal examination. Which of the following effects makes the drug useful for this purpose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Miosis from contraction of the sphincter pupillae

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mydriasis from stimulation of the iris dilator muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced intraocular pressure by increasing aqueous outflow

explanation.summary: Phenylephrine dilates the pupil by stimulating alpha-1 receptors on the iris dilator muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stimulation of alpha-1 receptors on the dilator muscle of the iris causes mydriasis, which is why phenylephrine is used as a diagnostic tool in ophthalmologic investigations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phenylephrine causes mydriasis (dilation), not miosis (constriction).
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick attributes its ophthalmic use to pupil dilation and vasoconstriction, not tear production.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Its ophthalmic role is pupil dilation for exams, not lowering intraocular pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine is not a corneal anesthetic.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements correctly contrasts alpha-1 and alpha-2 adrenergic receptors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alpha-1 receptors are presynaptic, while alpha-2 receptors are postsynaptic on vascular smooth muscle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alpha-1 receptors couple to Gq, while alpha-2 receptors are presynaptic and couple to Gi to reduce norepinephrine release

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both alpha-1 and alpha-2 receptors couple to Gs and raise cAMP

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alpha-2 receptors are widely distributed near the sympathetic terminal, while alpha-1 receptors regulate neurotransmitter release

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alpha-1 receptors decrease cytosolic calcium, while alpha-2 receptors increase it

explanation.summary: Alpha-1 receptors are postsynaptic Gq receptors; alpha-2 are presynaptic Gi receptors that curb norepinephrine release.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alpha-1 receptors are widely distributed in the immediate vicinity of the sympathetic terminal and are Gq-coupled, whereas alpha-2 receptors are presynaptic, couple to Gi, and regulate (reduce) the release of neurotransmitters such as norepinephrine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the locations; alpha-2 receptors are the presynaptic subtype.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha-1 receptors are Gq-coupled and alpha-2 are Gi-coupled; neither raises cAMP through Gs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This reverses the roles; alpha-1 receptors are near the terminal and alpha-2 receptors regulate neurotransmitter release.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alpha-1 activation increases cytosolic calcium through the Gq/IP3 pathway, not decreases it.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia is started on an α1-adrenergic blocker to relieve his urinary hesitancy and weak stream. The therapeutic effect of this drug class is achieved primarily by blocking α1-receptors on which of the following structures?

explanation.summary: α1-Blockade relieves BPH symptoms by relaxing prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: α1-Receptors are densely expressed in prostatic smooth muscle, and the α1A subtype in particular is abundant in the prostate and urinary tract. Blocking these receptors relaxes the smooth muscle, dilates the urethra, and decreases resistance to urine flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The adrenal gland has no notable α1-receptors relevant to this drug's action.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Although α-receptors exist in the cerebral cortex, blockade there is not clinically relevant to BPH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: α1-Blockade does not act on the liver.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Skeletal muscle is not a target of α1-adrenergic blockers.

questions.stem: A urologist wants to prescribe an α1-blocker whose selectivity for the α1A subtype makes it especially suited to treating benign prostatic hyperplasia. Which of the following agents is the α1A-selective (uroselective) blocker?

explanation.summary: Tamsulosin is the α1A-selective, uroselective blocker used for BPH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because α1A-receptor expression in the prostate and urinary tract is high, tamsulosin—an α1A blocker—is often prescribed for BPH. Its uroselectivity gives it strong effects on the lower urinary tract with relatively little effect on vascular tone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker used mainly for hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Terazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker, not α1A-selective.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Doxazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker used for hypertension and BPH.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phenoxybenzamine is a nonselective α (α1 and α2) antagonist used for pheochromocytoma.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia and a history of falls needs an α1-blocker that carries the lowest risk of orthostatic hypotension. Which of the following agents is the best choice?

explanation.summary: Tamsulosin has the lowest risk of orthostatic hypotension because of its α1A selectivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tamsulosin's selective affinity for α1A receptors in the lower urinary tract spares vascular α1-receptors, so it has the lowest risk of orthostatic hypotension. This makes it favorable in older patients prone to falls.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Terazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker that lowers blood pressure and can cause orthostatic hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prazosin has high affinity for α1-receptors and rapid onset, giving it the highest first-dose orthostatic hypotension risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doxazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker that decreases peripheral resistance and can cause orthostatic hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenoxybenzamine is a nonselective α-antagonist that markedly lowers blood pressure and is not used for BPH.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with episodic headaches, palpitations, and severe hypertension is diagnosed with pheochromocytoma and started on phenoxybenzamine before surgical resection. This drug is effective in pheochromocytoma because it acts as which of the following?

explanation.summary: Phenoxybenzamine is a nonselective (α1 and α2) antagonist used before pheochromocytoma surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: By blocking both α1 and α2 receptors, phenoxybenzamine decreases total peripheral resistance and blood pressure, countering the catecholamine surge from the tumor. Its irreversible binding gives longer, more pronounced effects than reversible agents.

whyWrongByChoice.A: It is not limited to α1; it also blocks α2, which is central to its use here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It is not α1-selective; nonselective α-blockade is what controls the catecholamine surge.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Selective α2-blockade would not effectively lower the catecholamine-driven vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: It is not a β-blocker; β-blockade must not be given first in pheochromocytoma.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is started on prazosin for hypertension. Shortly after his first dose he stands up and becomes lightheaded and nearly faints. Which of the following best explains this reaction?

choices.label: B
choices.text: α2-Agonism reduces norepinephrine release in the CNS, causing sedation

choices.label: C
choices.text: β1-Blockade slows the heart rate excessively

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iris dilator muscle atrophy reduces cerebral perfusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bladder smooth muscle relaxation reduces venous return

explanation.summary: Prazosin's first-dose orthostatic hypotension results from α1-mediated vasodilation with an inadequate baroreflex response.
explanation.whyCorrect: α1-Blockers inhibit vascular smooth muscle contraction, causing vasodilation. The compensatory baroreflex fails to sufficiently increase sympathetic outflow, so blood pressure drops on standing, producing syncope and dizziness. Prazosin's high α1 affinity and rapid onset make this most pronounced with the first dose.

whyWrongByChoice.B: α2-Agonism describes centrally acting drugs like clonidine, not the mechanism of prazosin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prazosin is an α1-blocker and does not block β1-receptors or slow the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iris dilator atrophy causes floppy iris syndrome, not orthostatic hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bladder relaxation causes ejaculatory problems, not a drop in venous return or blood pressure.

questions.stem: A patient with pheochromocytoma is being prepared for surgery. A trainee suggests starting a β-blocker first to control the tachycardia. The attending warns this order is dangerous. Which of the following best explains why α-blockade must precede β-blockade?

choices.label: A
choices.text: β-Blockade first removes β-mediated vasodilation, leaving unopposed α-mediated vasoconstriction

choices.label: C
choices.text: α-Blockers are ineffective once β-receptors are blocked

choices.label: D
choices.text: β-Blockade first causes reflex bradycardia that stops the tumor from secreting catecholamines

choices.label: E
choices.text: α-Blockade increases catecholamine release, which β-blockade then suppresses

explanation.summary: In pheochromocytoma, α-blockade must come before β-blockade to avoid unopposed α vasoconstriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Giving a β-blocker first knocks out the vasodilatory effect of β-adrenergic receptors while circulating catecholamines still stimulate α1-receptors. The result is unopposed α-mediated vasoconstriction and a hypertensive crisis, so α-blockade is initiated first, followed by β-blockade.

whyWrongByChoice.B: β-Blockers do not irreversibly block α2-receptors; this is not the concern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: α-Blockers remain effective; the issue is the hemodynamic consequence of β-blockade given first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: β-Blockade does not stop the tumor from secreting catecholamines.
whyWrongByChoice.E: α-Blockade does not increase catecholamine release in the way described; the danger is unopposed vasoconstriction from β-first dosing.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man undergoing cataract surgery develops a billowing, poorly dilating iris that prolapses during the procedure (intraoperative floppy iris syndrome). This complication is most strongly associated with chronic use of which of the following drugs?

explanation.summary: Tamsulosin carries the highest risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: IFIS is caused by inhibition of iris dilator smooth muscle contraction and atrophy from α1-receptor blockade, most often seen in cataract surgery. Tamsulosin is particularly associated with IFIS and carries the highest risk of any α1-blocker.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Prazosin can cause floppy iris but is far less associated with it than tamsulosin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phentolamine is a nonselective α-antagonist used for pheochromocytoma, not linked to IFIS in this way.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doxazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker with lower IFIS risk than tamsulosin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenoxybenzamine is used for pheochromocytoma and is not the classic IFIS culprit.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man taking tamsulosin for BPH reports that during orgasm he produces little or no semen, and his urine afterward appears cloudy. Which of the following best explains this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relaxation of bladder smooth muscle allows semen to be propelled backward into the bladder

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prolonged smooth muscle contraction traps semen in the prostate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased sympathetic tone closes the internal urethral sphincter

choices.label: D
choices.text: α2-Receptor stimulation blocks emission of semen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasodilation of penile arteries prevents erection

explanation.summary: α1-Blockers like tamsulosin can cause retrograde ejaculation by relaxing bladder smooth muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Some α1-blockers (e.g., tamsulosin, silodosin) relax the bladder and impair the smooth-muscle contraction that propels semen forward. Without forward propulsion, semen is directed backward into the bladder—retrograde ejaculation—explaining the cloudy post-orgasm urine.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The drug relaxes rather than contracts smooth muscle, so semen is not trapped by contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: α1-Blockade relaxes, not tightens, the internal sphincter; increased sympathetic tone is not the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The effect is due to α1-blockade relaxing bladder smooth muscle, not α2-receptor stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This describes an erectile effect, not the retrograde ejaculation the patient is experiencing.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man taking tamsulosin for BPH asks his physician about starting sildenafil for erectile dysfunction. The physician cautions that combining these drugs can be dangerous. Which of the following adverse outcomes is of greatest concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe hypotension with possible loss of consciousness

explanation.summary: Combining an α1-blocker with a PDE-5 inhibitor risks severe additive hypotension.
explanation.whyCorrect: α1-Blockers with other vasodilatory agents can cause significant hypotension. Concomitant use with a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor such as sildenafil can precipitate severe hypotension, which may lead to loss of consciousness.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The combined vasodilatory effect lowers, not raises, blood pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: α1-Blockers relieve urinary retention in BPH rather than cause it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The primary danger is hypotension from additive vasodilation, not a tachyarrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Priapism is a rare α1-blocker effect but is not the drug-interaction concern with sildenafil described here.

questions.stem: A man with benign prostatic hyperplasia reports urinary hesitancy, a weak stream, and incomplete voiding. His physician explains that α1-blockers improve these symptoms. Which of the following best describes how these drugs relieve his obstruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They relax prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle

choices.label: B
choices.text: They shrink the prostate gland by blocking androgen receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: They contract the detrusor muscle to forcefully expel urine

choices.label: D
choices.text: They increase urine production by acting on the kidney

choices.label: E
choices.text: They tighten the urethral sphincter to improve continence

explanation.summary: α1-Blockers treat BPH obstruction by relaxing prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: In BPH, α1-receptor activation in prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle causes contraction that obstructs the urethra. Blocking these receptors relaxes the smooth muscle, dilates the urethra, and decreases the resistance to urine flow, improving the urinary symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.B: α1-Blockers do not block androgen receptors or shrink the gland; that describes 5α-reductase inhibitors or antiandrogens.
whyWrongByChoice.C: They relax smooth muscle rather than contract the detrusor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: They act on lower urinary tract smooth muscle, not on renal urine production.
whyWrongByChoice.E: They relax rather than tighten the sphincter, reducing outflow resistance.

questions.stem: A pharmacology student reviews how α1-receptor blockade produces vasodilation. α1-Receptors are coupled to which of the following G proteins, whose downstream signaling is blocked by these drugs?

explanation.summary: α1-Receptors are Gq-coupled, signaling through Ca2+ and calmodulin-sensitive pathways.
explanation.whyCorrect: α1-Receptors belong to the Gq type of GPCR, and most of their actions are mediated by Ca2+ and calmodulin (CaM)-sensitive pathways that drive smooth-muscle contraction. Blocking α1 reduces this Ca2+/CaM-driven actin-myosin interaction, causing vasodilation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Gs is coupled to receptors such as β-adrenergic receptors, not α1.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gi is coupled to α2-receptors, which inhibit adenylyl cyclase, not α1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Golf is an olfactory G protein, unrelated to α1-adrenergic signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gt (transducin) mediates phototransduction in the retina, not α1 signaling.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man has both stage 1 hypertension and mild benign prostatic hyperplasia. His physician wants a single nonselective α1-blocker that is approved to treat both conditions. Which of the following agents best fits?

explanation.summary: Doxazosin is a nonselective α1-blocker approved for both hypertension and BPH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Non-uroselective α1-blockers such as prazosin, terazosin, and doxazosin are used as antihypertensive agents. Doxazosin (along with terazosin) has additionally been approved for the treatment of BPH, making it suitable for a patient with both conditions.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Tamsulosin is an α1A-selective uroselective agent for BPH; it is not used as an antihypertensive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phenoxybenzamine is a nonselective α-antagonist reserved for pheochromocytoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phentolamine is a nonselective α-antagonist used for short-term BP control in pheochromocytoma, not chronic hypertension plus BPH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Silodosin is a uroselective agent for BPH, not an antihypertensive.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman at 30 weeks' gestation presents with regular painful uterine contractions consistent with preterm labor. Her physician wants to relax the uterine smooth muscle to delay delivery. Which of the following medications is most appropriate for tocolysis?

explanation.summary: Terbutaline is a beta-2 agonist that relaxes uterine smooth muscle and is used off-label as a tocolytic in preterm labor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Terbutaline is a beta-2 agonist. Because beta-2 receptor activation relaxes smooth muscle, terbutaline relaxes the uterus and is used off-label as a tocolytic agent to slow preterm contractions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epinephrine is a nonselective adrenergic agonist and is not used to relax the uterus for tocolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Isoproterenol has strong beta-2 activity but is used for bradycardia and heart block, not as a tocolytic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phenylephrine is an alpha-1 agonist that causes smooth muscle contraction, the opposite of the desired effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dobutamine is a beta-1 agonist inotrope used in heart failure, with no role in tocolysis.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man in the cardiac ICU has acute decompensated heart failure with poor cardiac output. The team wants to increase myocardial contractility to restore adequate circulation. Which of the following agents primarily acts on beta-1 receptors to achieve this effect?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is a predominantly beta-1 agonist inotrope used to increase contractility in acute heart failure and cardiogenic shock.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dobutamine has predominant beta-1 agonist action (beta-1 >> beta-2). Beta-1 stimulation raises cAMP and intracellular calcium, increasing both contractility (inotropy) and heart rate (chronotropy), which restores circulation in acute care settings like heart failure and cardiogenic shock.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol is a beta-2-selective bronchodilator, not an inotrope.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Salmeterol is a long-acting beta-2 agonist used for airway maintenance, not cardiac support.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mirabegron is a beta-3 agonist that relaxes bladder detrusor muscle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Terbutaline is a beta-2 agonist used for bronchospasm and tocolysis, not for increasing contractility.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with asthma has been using her albuterol inhaler frequently during an exacerbation. She reports fine shaking of her hands. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stimulation of beta-2 receptors in skeletal muscle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blockade of muscarinic receptors in skeletal muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction of skeletal muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-1 stimulation of cardiac pacemaker cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Depletion of intracellular potassium in neurons

explanation.summary: Muscle tremor is a common adverse effect of beta-2 agonists due to stimulation of beta-2 receptors in skeletal muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-2 agonists such as albuterol stimulate beta-2 receptors located in skeletal muscle, and this is a very common cause of fine tremor with their use.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist, not a muscarinic antagonist, and antimuscarinic action does not explain tremor here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Albuterol is beta-2 selective and does not cause alpha-1 vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-1 cardiac stimulation causes tachycardia and palpitations, not skeletal muscle tremor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-2 agonists shift potassium into cells (lowering serum potassium) but this does not directly cause tremor via neuronal potassium depletion.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman reports urinary urgency and frequency consistent with overactive bladder. Her physician prescribes a drug that relaxes the detrusor smooth muscle during the storage phase to increase bladder capacity. Which of the following drugs was most likely prescribed?

explanation.summary: Mirabegron is a beta-3 agonist that relaxes detrusor smooth muscle and enhances bladder storage in overactive bladder.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mirabegron activates beta-3 receptors in the bladder, relaxing the detrusor smooth muscle during the urine storage phase and thereby increasing bladder capacity, which is why it is used for overactive bladder.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist bronchodilator, not a bladder agent.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dobutamine is a beta-1 inotrope used for cardiac support.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Isoproterenol is a nonselective beta agonist used for bradycardia/heart block, not overactive bladder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Terbutaline is a beta-2 agonist used for bronchospasm and tocolysis, not bladder storage.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic kidney disease presents with a serum potassium of 6.8 mEq/L. Along with other therapies, he is given nebulized albuterol. Which of the following best explains how albuterol lowers his serum potassium?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It increases renal excretion of potassium in the distal tubule

choices.label: B
choices.text: It activates the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, driving potassium into cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: It binds potassium within the gastrointestinal tract

choices.label: D
choices.text: It inhibits aldosterone at the mineralocorticoid receptor

choices.label: E
choices.text: It induces a metabolic acidosis that shifts potassium out of cells

explanation.summary: Beta-2 agonists activate the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, shifting potassium into cells and transiently lowering serum potassium.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-2 agonists such as albuterol activate the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, which drives sodium out of and potassium into the cell. This shift removes excess potassium from the serum, so albuterol can be used as a temporizing treatment for hyperkalemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol shifts potassium intracellularly rather than increasing renal excretion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Binding potassium in the gut describes potassium binders, not the beta-2 mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Albuterol does not act as an aldosterone antagonist.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A metabolic acidosis would shift potassium out of cells and raise serum potassium, the opposite of the observed effect.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man with asthma has worsening symptoms despite an as-needed rescue inhaler. His physician adds a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) for maintenance control. Which of the following drugs was most likely added?

explanation.summary: Salmeterol is a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) used for maintenance therapy as disease worsens or becomes resistant.
explanation.whyCorrect: Salmeterol is classified as a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA). LABAs are used for maintenance when disease worsens or begins to become resistant to treatment, in contrast to short-acting rescue agents.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA) used as a rescue inhaler, not a maintenance LABA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Terbutaline is a short-acting beta-2 agonist and is used off-label for tocolysis, not as a maintenance LABA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epinephrine is a nonselective adrenergic agonist, not a maintenance inhaler for asthma control.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dobutamine is a beta-1 inotrope with no role in asthma.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man in the ICU is receiving an intravenous vasopressor through a peripheral line. The nurse notices the drug has infiltrated the surrounding tissue, which is now pale and cool from local vasoconstriction, raising concern for skin necrosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate agent to inject subcutaneously to limit tissue damage?

explanation.summary: Subcutaneous phentolamine, an alpha-antagonist, reverses the local vasoconstriction caused by vasopressor extravasation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Extravasation of a vasopressor causes severe local vasoconstriction that can lead to skin necrosis. Injecting phentolamine, an alpha-antagonist, subcutaneously helps reduce the local vasoconstriction and prevent tissue injury. Central venous administration is preferred to avoid this problem.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Propranolol is a beta-blocker and does not reverse alpha-mediated local vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atropine is a muscarinic antagonist and does not address the vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epinephrine would worsen vasoconstriction through alpha-1 stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Isoproterenol is a beta agonist and is not the antidote for local extravasation-induced vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman uses an albuterol inhaler for acute bronchospasm. Which of the following best describes the receptor and downstream signaling that produce bronchial smooth muscle relaxation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activation of a Gq receptor that raises IP3 and cytosolic calcium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blockade of muscarinic (M3) receptors on bronchial smooth muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stimulation of a Gs receptor that raises cAMP and lowers cytosolic calcium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Activation of a Gi receptor that lowers cAMP and PKA activity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct opening of voltage-gated calcium channels

explanation.summary: Beta-2 receptors are Gs-coupled; activation raises cAMP and lowers cytosolic calcium, relaxing bronchial smooth muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle are Gs-type receptors. Their activation increases adenylyl cyclase activity and cAMP, which decreases cytosolic calcium and relaxes the smooth muscle, producing bronchodilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gq signaling raises IP3 and calcium, causing contraction, not the relaxation seen with beta-2 activation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist, not a muscarinic antagonist, although both can relieve bronchospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-2 receptors couple to Gs (raising cAMP), not Gi.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-2 agonists lower cytosolic calcium; they do not open voltage-gated calcium channels.

questions.stem: Beta-adrenergic receptors are G-protein coupled receptors that signal through the Gs pathway. Following activation of a Gs-coupled beta receptor, which of the following changes in intracellular signaling is expected?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased cAMP levels and increased PKA activation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased IP3 and increased cytosolic calcium

explanation.summary: Gs activation increases adenylyl cyclase activity, raising cAMP and activating PKA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gs activation increases adenylyl cyclase activity and cAMP levels, which in turn activates downstream PKA. In the heart this leads to increased calcium, contractility, and heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased cAMP is the action of the Gi pathway, the opposite of Gs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased IP3 and cytosolic calcium is the Gq pathway, not Gs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PKA activation increases with Gs signaling; it does not decrease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: cGMP is not the second messenger of the Gs pathway.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with symptomatic bradycardia and heart block is considered for a drug with both beta-1 and beta-2 activity (beta-2 stronger than beta-1) that can increase heart rate. Which of the following agents best fits this description?

explanation.summary: Isoproterenol is a nonselective beta agonist (beta-2 >> beta-1) whose beta-1 activity can help treat bradycardia and heart block.
explanation.whyCorrect: Isoproterenol (isoprenaline) has beta-2 activity stronger than beta-1 but retains beta-1 effects. Its beta-1 activity can be helpful in treating bradycardia and heart block, although its overall use is limited.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol is beta-2 selective and used for bronchodilation, not for heart block.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dobutamine is predominantly beta-1 selective, not a drug with stronger beta-2 activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mirabegron is a beta-3 agonist acting on the bladder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine is an alpha-1 agonist and is not a beta agonist.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with overactive bladder is being evaluated for mirabegron. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension. Which of the following adverse effects is most associated with this beta-3 agonist and should be considered before prescribing?

explanation.summary: Mirabegron commonly causes hypertension and urinary retention; it should be avoided in severe or uncontrolled hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: Given its beta-3 properties, mirabegron commonly causes urinary retention and hypertension. Because of the hypertensive effect, patients with severe or uncontrolled hypertension should not take mirabegron.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bronchospasm is not a characteristic adverse effect of the beta-3 agonist mirabegron.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypoglycemia is not associated with mirabegron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mirabegron is linked to hypertension rather than bradycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diarrhea is not the characteristic adverse effect being screened for here.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy with asthma is prescribed albuterol as an inhaler rather than an oral tablet. Which of the following best explains the rationale for using the inhaled route?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral albuterol is destroyed by stomach acid before absorption

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhalation delivers drug directly to bronchial smooth muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhalation increases systemic bioavailability compared with the oral route

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-2 receptors are absent from the gastrointestinal tract

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhalation converts a short-acting agent into a long-acting one

explanation.summary: Inhaled beta-2 agonists reach the bronchial smooth muscle directly for rapid onset with reduced systemic effects.
explanation.whyCorrect: The inhaler route provides an easy, convenient way to administer albuterol with rapid onset because the drug immediately reaches its site of action in the bronchial smooth muscle. It also decreases the risk of systemic side effects compared with an oral form, which would delay onset and undergo first-pass metabolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The rationale is direct delivery and reduced systemic exposure, not destruction of the drug by stomach acid.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An oral drug form would decrease bioavailability due to first-pass effect; inhalation is chosen for local action, not higher systemic bioavailability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-2 receptors are present in many tissues; the key point is targeting the airway directly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Route of administration does not change albuterol from a short-acting to a long-acting agent.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with hypertension is started on metoprolol. His blood pressure improves over the following weeks. Part of the antihypertensive effect is due to reduced secretion of a substance from the juxtaglomerular cells of the kidney. Decreased release of which of the following is most directly responsible?

explanation.summary: β1-receptors on renal juxtaglomerular cells stimulate renin release; blocking them lowers renin.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the kidney, β1-receptors stimulate renin release and activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Blocking these β1-receptors reduces renin secretion, which lowers angiotensin II, decreases water retention and vasoconstriction, and helps lower blood pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aldosterone falls only secondarily to reduced angiotensin II; it is not the substance released by juxtaglomerular cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ADH is released from the posterior pituitary, not the juxtaglomerular apparatus, and is not the primary target of β-blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial natriuretic peptide is secreted by atrial myocytes in response to stretch, not by juxtaglomerular cells under β1 control.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cortisol is an adrenal glucocorticoid unrelated to β1-mediated renin release.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with a history of asthma is prescribed propranolol for frequent migraines. Two hours after her first dose she develops wheezing, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. Which of the following best explains her symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: β1-receptor blockade in the sinoatrial node

choices.label: B
choices.text: β2-receptor blockade in bronchiolar smooth muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: α1-receptor blockade in the airway vasculature

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intrinsic sympathomimetic activity at bronchial β2-receptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of muscarinic receptors in the bronchi

explanation.summary: Nonselective β-blockers cause bronchoconstriction by blocking β2-receptors in the airways.
explanation.whyCorrect: β2-receptors in bronchiolar smooth muscle normally produce bronchodilation. A nonselective β-blocker such as propranolol blocks these receptors, causing bronchoconstriction that can be dangerous in patients with asthma or COPD. A β1-selective agent would be less likely to trigger this effect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: β1 blockade in the SA node slows heart rate but does not narrow the airways.
whyWrongByChoice.C: β-blockers like propranolol do not act on α1-receptors, and α1 blockade would not cause bronchoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Propranolol lacks intrinsic sympathomimetic activity; even if present, partial agonism would tend to relax, not constrict, the airway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: β-blockers do not block muscarinic receptors; antimuscarinics actually relieve bronchoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with type 2 diabetes managed with insulin develops hypertension requiring a β-blocker. His physician wants to minimize masking of hypoglycemic warning symptoms and avoid worsening glucose control. Which of the following agents is the most appropriate choice?

explanation.summary: β1-selective agents are preferred in insulin-treated diabetics to limit β2-mediated metabolic effects.
explanation.whyCorrect: β2-blockade can cause hypoglycemia and mask its adrenergic warning signs, which is dangerous in insulin-treated diabetics. Atenolol is β1-selective (cardioselective), so it spares β2-receptors and is preferred in patients prone to hypoglycemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Propranolol is nonselective, blocking β2-receptors and thereby masking hypoglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Timolol is a nonselective β-blocker and would not spare β2-receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nadolol is nonselective and carries the same β2-related metabolic risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carvedilol is a nonselective β-blocker with additional α1-blockade, not β1-selective.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man in the operating room develops acute-onset sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 150/min and rising blood pressure. The anesthesiologist wants a β-blocker that can be titrated intravenously with a very rapid onset and a short duration in case it must be stopped quickly. Which of the following is the most appropriate agent?

explanation.summary: Esmolol is an IV-only, β1-selective agent with ultra-short action due to esterase metabolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: Esmolol is a β1-selective blocker formulated for intravenous use only. It is rapidly hydrolyzed by blood esterases, giving an onset within about 5-10 minutes and a very short half-life, making it ideal for rapid, titratable rate control that can be reversed quickly by stopping the infusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atenolol is hydrophilic and renally cleared with a longer half-life, not suited for rapid on-off titration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nadolol is long-acting and nonselective, the opposite of what is needed here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carvedilol is an oral nonselective β/α-blocker used chronically, not for acute IV titration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pindolol has intrinsic sympathomimetic activity and is oral, not an ultra-short IV agent.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old professional violinist reports that anticipatory palpitations and a fine hand tremor impair her performances during high-stakes auditions. She has no cardiac or pulmonary disease. Her physician recommends a β-blocker taken shortly before performances. Which agent's high lipophilicity and central nervous system penetration make it a classic choice for this use?

explanation.summary: Propranolol is the most lipophilic β-blocker and is used prophylactically for performance anxiety.
explanation.whyCorrect: Propranolol is the most lipophilic β-blocker, has a high volume of distribution, and readily crosses the blood-brain barrier to elicit CNS effects. Blocking β-receptors dampens the sympathetic tremor and palpitations of performance anxiety when taken prophylactically.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atenolol is hydrophilic and penetrates the CNS poorly, making it less suited for anxiety symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Esmolol is an IV-only agent used for acute rate control, not oral prophylaxis of anxiety.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nadolol is relatively hydrophilic and long-acting and is not the classic anxiety agent.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nebivolol is a β1-selective agent used for hypertension, not the traditional choice for performance anxiety.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with Graves disease presents with a heart rate of 128/min, tremor, heat intolerance, and anxiety while awaiting definitive therapy. A medication is added to rapidly control her adrenergic symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate agent?

explanation.summary: Nonselective propranolol controls the sympathetic symptoms of hyperthyroidism.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hyperthyroidism the combined sympathetic effects drive tachycardia, tremor, and anxiety. β-blockade reduces this sympathetic overactivity, and the nonselective agent propranolol is used to control these symptoms while definitive thyroid treatment is arranged.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Timolol is used topically for glaucoma and is not the standard oral agent for symptomatic hyperthyroidism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Esmolol is an IV agent reserved for acute inpatient rate control, not routine symptom control in hyperthyroidism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Betaxolol is a β1-selective agent, whereas propranolol is the classic choice highlighted for hyperthyroidism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carvedilol is used in heart failure and hypertension, not as the standard agent for hyperthyroid symptoms.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with open-angle glaucoma is prescribed timolol eye drops. Which of the following best describes how this medication lowers intraocular pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased outflow of aqueous humor through the trabecular meshwork

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contraction of the ciliary muscle opening the canal of Schlemm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pupillary constriction widening the iridocorneal angle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Osmotic withdrawal of fluid from the vitreous

explanation.summary: Topical β-blockers like timolol lower intraocular pressure by reducing aqueous humor production.
explanation.whyCorrect: Timolol is a nonselective β-blocker used topically for glaucoma. β-blockade in the ciliary epithelium decreases the production of aqueous humor, thereby lowering intraocular pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing trabecular outflow is the mechanism of drugs like prostaglandin analogs or pilocarpine, not β-blockers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ciliary muscle contraction to open the canal of Schlemm is the action of cholinergic agonists such as pilocarpine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pupillary constriction to widen the angle describes cholinergic miotics, not β-blockers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Osmotic fluid withdrawal describes agents like mannitol, not topical timolol.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (ejection fraction 30%) is clinically stable on an ACE inhibitor and a diuretic. His cardiologist adds a β-blocker to improve long-term survival. Which of the following agents is specifically indicated for this purpose?

explanation.summary: Carvedilol is one of the β-blockers shown to improve mortality in chronic heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: In heart failure, reduced cardiac output triggers chronic sympathetic activation that worsens the disease. β-blockers such as carvedilol blunt this harmful activation and have been shown to improve mortality and morbidity, so the potential negative inotropic effect is outweighed by long-term benefit in a stable patient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Propranolol is not among the β-blockers demonstrated to reduce mortality in heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting IV agent for acute rate control, not chronic heart failure therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Timolol is used topically for glaucoma and has no role in heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pindolol has intrinsic sympathomimetic activity and is not used to improve survival in heart failure.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with stable angina has been taking propranolol for years. After running out of his prescription, he abruptly stops the drug and over the next few days experiences worsening chest pain, more frequent anginal episodes, and palpitations. Which of the following best explains this rebound phenomenon?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accumulation of an active hepatic metabolite

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of coronary vasospasm from α-blockade

explanation.summary: Abrupt β-blocker withdrawal causes rebound because chronically blocked β1-receptors have upregulated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic β-blockade leads to upregulation and increased sensitivity of β1-receptors. Abruptly stopping the drug exposes these upregulated, hypersensitive receptors to circulating catecholamines, producing rebound tachycardia and exacerbation of angina. β-blockers should therefore be tapered gradually.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Rebound is due to receptor changes, not accumulation of an active metabolite, and propranolol has a short half-life.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic blockade causes upregulation, not downregulation, of β1-receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Propranolol lacks intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, so there is none to be unmasked.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Propranolol does not block α-receptors, so α-mediated coronary vasospasm does not explain the withdrawal syndrome.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man presents to the emergency department with chest pain and hypertension after using cocaine recreationally. The team wants to avoid a nonselective β-blocker such as propranolol in this setting. Which of the following best explains the concern?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Precipitation of hyperglycemia from β2 stimulation

explanation.summary: Blocking β2-mediated vasodilation leaves α-vasoconstriction unopposed, dangerous in cocaine toxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cocaine produces adrenergic stimulation of both α and β receptors. A nonselective β-blocker removes β2-mediated vasodilation of vessels such as skeletal muscle arteries, leaving α-adrenergic vasoconstriction unopposed. This can worsen coronary and systemic vasoconstriction and precipitate a hypertensive/ischemic crisis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: β-blockade causes bronchoconstriction, not excessive bronchodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: β-blockers reduce renin release; they do not cause reflex renin-driven hypotension here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: β2 blockade tends to promote hypoglycemia, not hyperglycemia, and this is not the concern in cocaine toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute receptor downregulation is not the mechanism of harm; unopposed α vasoconstriction is.

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is comparing two β-blockers. Drug X is described as having intrinsic sympathomimetic activity and acting as a partial agonist, providing low-grade β-stimulation at rest while behaving as a blocker when sympathetic tone is high. Because of this property, Drug X is specifically noted to be a poor choice in patients with angina. Which of the following agents is Drug X?

explanation.summary: Pindolol has intrinsic sympathomimetic (partial agonist) activity and is avoided in angina.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pindolol (like acebutolol) has intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, providing partial agonist stimulation at rest but blocking when sympathetic activity is high. This low-grade stimulation can exacerbate sympathetic tone and precipitate anginal episodes, so these agents are contraindicated in angina.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metoprolol is a β1-selective blocker without intrinsic sympathomimetic activity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atenolol is a hydrophilic β1-selective blocker lacking intrinsic sympathomimetic activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting β1-selective agent without partial agonist activity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nadolol is a long-acting nonselective blocker but does not have intrinsic sympathomimetic activity.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is started on metoprolol two days after a myocardial infarction. His cardiologist explains that the drug helps protect the heart. Which of the following best describes how β1-blockade benefits the myocardium in ischemic heart disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It increases coronary artery diameter through direct vasodilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases myocardial contractility to improve perfusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: It raises heart rate to improve cardiac output

explanation.summary: In ischemia, β1-blockade lowers heart rate and contractility, reducing myocardial oxygen demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ischemic heart disease is a mismatch between the oxygen supply and demand of cardiac muscle. The negative inotropic and chronotropic effects of β1-blockade decrease myocardial contractility and heart rate, lowering oxygen demand and alleviating the mismatch. Cardioselective agents such as metoprolol are used after myocardial infarction for this reason.

whyWrongByChoice.A: β-blockers do not directly dilate coronary arteries; the benefit comes from reduced oxygen demand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: β-blockers decrease, not increase, myocardial contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.D: β-blockers do not have thrombolytic activity and cannot dissolve a clot.
whyWrongByChoice.E: β-blockers lower heart rate; raising it would increase oxygen demand and worsen ischemia.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is given a cholinergic agonist during a study of autonomic reflexes, and his heart rate slows. Activation of which of the following signaling pathways in cardiac tissue is most directly responsible for this effect?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gq-coupled receptor that increases IP3 and intracellular calcium

explanation.summary: Cardiac slowing by cholinergics is mediated by the M2 receptor, a Gi-coupled GPCR that lowers cAMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the heart, muscarinic M2 receptors couple to Gi, decreasing cAMP and reducing rate and AV nodal conduction. This is why parasympathetic (cholinergic) activation slows the pulse.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gs increases cAMP and would raise, not lower, heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gq with increased IP3/Ca2+ is the M1/M3 pathway for glands and smooth muscle, not the cardiac slowing pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ligand-gated Na+/K+ channels describe nicotinic (Nm) receptors at the neuromuscular junction, not cardiac muscarinic signaling.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Muscarinic receptors are GPCRs, not tyrosine kinase receptors.

questions.stem: Which muscarinic receptor subtype is primarily responsible for slowing the heart rate and impulse conduction through the cardiac nodes?

explanation.summary: The M2 receptor mediates cholinergic slowing of the heart.
explanation.whyCorrect: M2 is the dominant cardiac muscarinic receptor; via Gi it decreases cAMP and slows the pulse rate and conduction through the SA and AV nodes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: M1 is a Gq neuronal receptor associated with cognition, memory, and learning.
whyWrongByChoice.C: M3 is a Gq receptor driving gland secretion and smooth-muscle contraction, not cardiac slowing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: M5 is not the clinically important cardiac subtype; M1-M3 are the key clinical receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nm is the nicotinic ligand-gated channel at skeletal muscle, unrelated to nodal slowing.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man cannot void on the third postoperative day. There is no mechanical obstruction, and his bladder is markedly distended. A drug is chosen that contracts the detrusor muscle and relaxes the internal urethral sphincter to promote emptying. Which of the following is the most appropriate agent?

explanation.summary: Bethanechol is the direct-acting cholinergic used to treat non-obstructive urinary retention.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bethanechol is an orally administered choline ester that causes bladder detrusor contraction and relaxes the urinary sphincter, treating urinary retention (and also ileus).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atropine is an antimuscarinic; it relaxes the detrusor and would worsen retention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pilocarpine is used for xerostomia and glaucoma, not urinary retention.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carbachol is used ophthalmically for glaucoma, not for bladder emptying.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetylcholine is rapidly hydrolyzed by acetylcholinesterase and is not used therapeutically for retention.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with Sjogren syndrome has severe dry mouth that is also worse after prior head and neck radiation. She is started on an oral agent that stimulates salivary secretion. Which of the following was most likely prescribed?

explanation.summary: Pilocarpine is used orally to treat xerostomia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pilocarpine, a tertiary-amine alkaloid, is used for xerostomia after head and neck radiation and in Sjogren syndrome, where disrupted lacrimal and salivary function improves with cholinergic stimulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bethanechol is used for urinary retention and ileus, not xerostomia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Carbachol is used as ophthalmic drops for glaucoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Methacholine is a diagnostic bronchoprovocation agent with no other therapeutic use.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine is an antimuscarinic that would reduce secretions and worsen dry mouth.

questions.stem: A patient with glaucoma is treated with cholinergic eye drops. The reduction in intraocular pressure is best explained by which of the following drug-induced effects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contraction of the pupillary sphincter and ciliary muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relaxation of the bladder detrusor muscle

explanation.summary: Cholinergic drops lower intraocular pressure by contracting the pupillary sphincter and ciliary muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carbachol and pilocarpine cause miosis by contracting the pupillary sphincter and adjust the ciliary muscle, reducing resistance to aqueous humor outflow and thereby lowering intraocular pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cholinergics cause miosis, not mydriasis; mydriasis is an antimuscarinic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bladder effects are unrelated to the ocular mechanism of pressure reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cholinergics increase, not decrease, lacrimal secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cholinergics cause bronchoconstriction, not bronchodilation.

questions.stem: Indirect-acting cholinomimetic agents increase parasympathetic activity through which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Directly binding and activating muscarinic receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocking reuptake of norepinephrine at the synapse

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing synthesis of choline in the presynaptic neuron

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antagonizing nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction

explanation.summary: Indirect-acting cholinomimetics are cholinesterase inhibitors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike direct-acting agents that bind receptors, indirect-acting cholinomimetics inhibit acetylcholinesterase, preventing breakdown of ACh and thereby increasing PNS activation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: That describes direct-acting cholinomimetics, not the indirect class.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blocking norepinephrine reuptake is an adrenergic action unrelated to cholinergic breakdown.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increasing choline synthesis is not the defined mechanism of these drugs.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antagonizing nicotinic receptors would reduce, not increase, cholinergic transmission.

questions.stem: Compared with acetylcholine, choline ester drugs such as carbachol have a longer duration of action. Which of the following properties best explains this difference?

choices.label: A
