choices.text: Greater resistance to hydrolysis by acetylcholinesterase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid renal excretion of the active drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: Irreversible covalent binding to the receptor

explanation.summary: Choline esters are longer-acting because they resist acetylcholinesterase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Modification of the choline side chain makes esters like carbachol more resistant to hydrolysis by acetylcholinesterase than ACh, giving a longer duration of action. Methacholine is the exception, being rapidly hydrolyzed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Rapid hydrolysis would shorten, not lengthen, duration; this describes ACh (and methacholine).
whyWrongByChoice.C: These drugs do bind and activate muscarinic receptors, which is required for their effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Duration is explained by enzymatic resistance, not renal excretion in this brick.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Their action is receptor activation, not irreversible covalent binding.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with intermittent cough and suspected asthma undergoes pulmonary function testing. An inhaled agent is administered to provoke bronchoconstriction as a diagnostic challenge. Which of the following was most likely used?

explanation.summary: Methacholine is used for bronchoprovocation testing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methacholine is indicated for testing bronchospastic (bronchoprovocation) activity as a diagnostic tool and, unlike the other choline esters, is rapidly hydrolyzed and has no other therapeutic use.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bethanechol is used for urinary retention and ileus, not bronchial testing.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pilocarpine is used for xerostomia and glaucoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carbachol is an ophthalmic agent for glaucoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine is an antimuscarinic and would block, not provoke, bronchoconstriction.

questions.stem: Activation of muscarinic receptors in the gastrointestinal tract produces which of the following effects?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contraction of the internal anal sphincter

explanation.summary: Cholinergic activation increases GI motility and secretions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Muscarinic (M3) activation increases gastric motility and secretions and relaxes the internal anal sphincter; the increased motility can cause diarrhea.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased motility, not decreased, is the cholinergic effect, so constipation is incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gastric secretions increase with cholinergic activation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The internal anal sphincter relaxes rather than contracts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Salivation increases with cholinergic activation.

questions.stem: Cholinergic activation of the eye produces which of the following effects?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Miosis and contraction of the ciliary muscle

explanation.summary: Cholinergic activation of the eye causes miosis and ciliary contraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Muscarinic activation contracts the sphincter and ciliary muscles of the eye, producing miosis (pupil constriction) and increased lacrimation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mydriasis is an antimuscarinic effect, the opposite of cholinergic action.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cholinergic activation increases lacrimation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pupillary sphincter contracts, not relaxes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cholinergic drugs lower intraocular pressure, which is why they treat glaucoma.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with Sjogren syndrome is treated with oral pilocarpine. Which of the following adverse effects is she most likely to report, occurring in roughly half of patients?

explanation.summary: Pilocarpine commonly causes excessive sweating.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pilocarpine is associated with excess sweating; about 50-60% of patients report this adverse effect due to cholinergic stimulation of sweat glands.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cholinergics increase GI motility and tend toward diarrhea, not constipation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cholinergics cause miosis, not mydriasis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Muscarinic activation slows the heart, producing bradycardia rather than tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cholinergics increase secretions and sweating, so dry skin is incorrect.

questions.stem: Pilocarpine differs from choline ester cholinergics in that it is a tertiary-amine alkaloid. Which of the following properties does this structure confer?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is able to cross the blood-brain barrier

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can only be administered intravenously

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is rapidly degraded by acetylcholinesterase

explanation.summary: As a tertiary-amine alkaloid, pilocarpine crosses the blood-brain barrier.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that pilocarpine is a tertiary amine that does cross the blood-brain barrier, distinguishing it from the choline esters.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pilocarpine is a muscarinic agonist and does activate these receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pilocarpine is given orally and ophthalmically, not only IV.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Its alkaloid structure is not a substrate rapidly cleaved by acetylcholinesterase like ACh.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pilocarpine acts on muscarinic receptors, not selectively on nicotinic receptors.

questions.stem: A pharmacologist is describing how a class of drugs produces its effects. The drugs bind muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the peripheral nervous system and CNS but leave nicotinic receptors unaffected, and their actions are opposite to those of the rest-and-digest parasympathetic system. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of these drugs?

explanation.summary: Anticholinergics (antimuscarinics) act by competitive inhibition of muscarinic receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that anticholinergics act primarily through competitive inhibition of muscarinic receptors in the PNS, CNS, or both, and that most do not block nicotinic receptors. Their effects are therefore opposite to those of parasympathetic (cholinergic) stimulation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anticholinergics generally do not block nicotinic receptors, and their muscarinic blockade is competitive and reversible.
whyWrongByChoice.C: They antagonize rather than activate muscarinic receptors; agonism would produce cholinergic (parasympathetic) effects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting acetylcholinesterase raises acetylcholine and produces cholinergic effects, the opposite of an anticholinergic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticholinergics target muscarinic, not adrenergic, receptors.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man in the emergency department has symptomatic sinus bradycardia with a heart rate of 38/min and lightheadedness. He is given intravenous atropine, and his heart rate rises to 74/min. Blockade of which receptor best explains this increase in heart rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: M3 muscarinic receptors on glandular tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: M2 muscarinic receptors on the heart

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-1 adrenergic receptors on the heart

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction

choices.label: E
choices.text: M3 muscarinic receptors on airway smooth muscle

explanation.summary: Atropine speeds the heart by blocking cardiac M2 muscarinic receptors, removing vagal slowing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick's organ table lists the heart's effect as increased heart rate mediated by M2 receptors. Anticholinergics inhibit the parasympathetic (vagal) slowing of the heart, producing tachycardia, which is why atropine is useful in symptomatic bradycardia and AV node block.

whyWrongByChoice.A: M3 receptors on glands mediate secretions (dry mouth), not heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atropine is a muscarinic antagonist and does not act on beta-1 adrenergic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neuromuscular nicotinic receptors control skeletal muscle, not the heart rate, and are not blocked by atropine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Airway M3 receptors govern bronchial tone, not cardiac rate.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is planning a week-long ocean cruise and reports severe seasickness on prior trips. Her physician prescribes a transdermal patch that must be replaced after 72 hours. Which anticholinergic drug is most likely being prescribed?

explanation.summary: Scopolamine, a transdermal-patch anticholinergic, is used for motion sickness.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states scopolamine (hyoscine) is usually formulated as a transdermal patch to treat motion sickness and postsurgical nausea, and that the patches are not for continuous use and should be replaced after 72 hours.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atropine is used for bradycardia, reducing secretions, and as a poisoning antidote, not as a motion-sickness patch.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ipratropium is an inhaled agent for asthma and COPD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxybutynin treats urinary urge incontinence.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tropicamide is an ophthalmic drop used to dilate the pupil.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with COPD is prescribed an inhaled anticholinergic to add to his beta-agonist and inhaled corticosteroid. By blocking M3 receptors in the airways, this drug relieves his symptoms. Which effect on the airways is responsible for the benefit?

explanation.summary: Inhaled anticholinergics such as ipratropium cause bronchodilation and reduce mucus secretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that M3 receptors in the lungs can cause bronchoconstriction; blocking them with ipratropium or tiotropium reduces mucus secretions and bronchoconstriction, lowering airway resistance and allowing easier breathing in asthma and COPD.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bronchoconstriction and increased secretions are the parasympathetic effects that anticholinergics oppose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The benefit comes from bronchodilation and decreased secretions, not increased ciliary clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The brick attributes the effect to airway smooth muscle and secretions, not pulmonary vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surfactant production is not a described mechanism of these drugs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has a sudden, frequent urge to urinate with occasional involuntary leakage, and urodynamic testing shows detrusor overactivity. She is started on oxybutynin. What is the mechanism by which this drug relieves her symptoms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocking muscarinic receptors to slow detrusor contractions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blocking alpha-adrenergic receptors in the bladder neck

explanation.summary: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic that slows detrusor contractions to control urge incontinence.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes oxybutynin as the prototypical anticholinergic for urinary incontinence (overactive bladder); by blocking M3 receptors it slows bladder detrusor contractions, helping patients control urine output.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stimulating detrusor contraction would worsen urgency and leakage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticholinergics reduce detrusor overactivity; they do not work by relaxing the urethral sphincter.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The drug does not increase urine production; it reduces involuntary bladder contractions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oxybutynin is antimuscarinic, not an alpha-adrenergic blocker.

questions.stem: An ophthalmologist needs to dilate a patient's pupil to examine the retina during a funduscopic exam. Which of the following anticholinergic agents, administered as an eye drop, is most appropriate for producing mydriasis?

explanation.summary: Anticholinergic drops such as tropicamide (or atropine) dilate the pupil for eye exams.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that atropine and tropicamide drops are used to dilate the pupil for ophthalmoscopic exams; the mydriasis helps with retinal and pupillary examination.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Scopolamine is used as a transdermal patch for motion sickness, not as an ophthalmic dilating drop.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ipratropium is an inhaled airway agent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxybutynin treats overactive bladder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycopyrrolate does not penetrate well and is not described as an ophthalmic mydriatic agent.

questions.stem: A toxicology instructor teaches the classic anticholinergic toxidrome mnemonic. A poisoned patient who is described as 'mad as a hatter' is exhibiting which of the following?

explanation.summary: 'Mad as a hatter' refers to the CNS effects of anticholinergic toxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick maps 'mad as a hatter' to central nervous system effects such as drowsiness, hallucinations, and coma, which result from central muscarinic blockade in agents that penetrate the CNS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mydriasis and blurred vision correspond to 'blind as a bat'.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tachycardia (and overheating) corresponds to 'hot as a hare'.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dry mouth and decreased secretions correspond to 'dry as a bone'.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flushed skin corresponds to 'red as a beet'.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man presents with severe lower abdominal pain and states he has not urinated in several days. He has no prior urinary problems and is generally healthy but recently began an over-the-counter allergy medicine for a runny nose. Examination reveals a markedly distended bladder requiring catheterization. Which property of his allergy medication best explains this presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It contains diphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic properties

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a direct cholinergic agonist that stimulates the detrusor

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is an alpha-adrenergic antagonist that relaxes the bladder neck

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is a diuretic that overfilled the bladder

explanation.summary: OTC diphenhydramine has anticholinergic properties that can cause urinary retention, especially in the elderly.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick's case describes exactly this scenario: the allergy medicine contains diphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. By blocking muscarinic receptors it decreases detrusor contraction and causes urinary retention, a particular risk in elderly patients who also face confusion, constipation, and falls.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A cholinergic agonist would promote urination, not cause retention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An acetylcholinesterase inhibitor increases acetylcholine and cholinergic activity, opposite to what caused retention.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alpha-blockade at the bladder neck would ease urine flow, not cause retention.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A diuretic would increase urine output; the problem was inability to void due to anticholinergic effect.

questions.stem: A patient who took a large amount of an anticholinergic agent is brought to the emergency department. On the anticholinergic toxidrome mnemonic, the finding 'dry as a bone' reflects which underlying mechanism?

explanation.summary: 'Dry as a bone' reflects inhibition of secretions, producing dry mouth, dry eyes, and decreased sweating.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick attributes 'dry as a bone' to the general inhibition of secretions in mucous membranes and skin, giving dry mouth, dry eyes, and reduced sweating from muscarinic blockade of exocrine glands.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pupillary dilation corresponds to 'blind as a bat'.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blockade of cardiac vagal tone produces tachycardia ('hot as a hare').
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased gut motility contributes to 'full as a flask' (retention/constipation).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cutaneous flushing corresponds to 'red as a beet'.

questions.stem: A farm worker is brought in after exposure to an organophosphate insecticide, with copious secretions, bradycardia, and pinpoint pupils. Which anticholinergic drug is the appropriate antidote for this acetylcholine-excess (muscarinic) toxicity?

explanation.summary: Atropine is the antidote for acetylcholine toxicity, such as organophosphate or muscarine poisoning.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states atropine is an antidote for acetylcholine toxicity, including poisoning by organophosphates or muscarine-containing wild mushrooms. By competitively blocking muscarinic receptors, it reverses the excess-cholinergic secretions, bradycardia, and miosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Oxybutynin is a bladder-selective agent for overactive bladder, not a poisoning antidote.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Scopolamine is used for motion sickness, not as the antidote of choice for cholinergic crisis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tropicamide is an ophthalmic drop for pupil dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ipratropium is an inhaled airway agent for asthma and COPD.

questions.stem: A student is asked to predict adverse effects of anticholinergic drugs from their receptor actions. Which of the following is a likely adverse effect of an anticholinergic agent?

explanation.summary: Anticholinergics decrease GI motility, which can lead to constipation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick's review question confirms constipation as a correct answer: anticholinergics inhibit gut smooth muscle contraction and decrease motility, which can cause constipation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anticholinergics decrease secretions, causing dry mouth, not increased salivation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: They decrease, not increase, GI motility.
whyWrongByChoice.D: They cause mydriasis (dilation), not miosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: They cause tachycardia by blocking vagal tone, not bradycardia.

questions.stem: A neurologist notes that some antimuscarinic drugs are useful for CNS diseases while others are not, depending on how well they penetrate the brain. Which of the following best explains why ipratropium and glycopyrrolate are NOT useful in treating Parkinson disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They do not adequately cross into the central nervous system

choices.label: B
choices.text: They block nicotinic rather than muscarinic receptors

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are rapidly metabolized before absorption

choices.label: E
choices.text: They stimulate rather than block muscarinic receptors

explanation.summary: CNS penetration varies among antimuscarinics; poorly penetrating agents cannot treat central diseases.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that muscarinic antagonists have variable CNS penetration. Certain agents (e.g., benztropine, atropine) carry greater CNS effects, whereas ipratropium and glycopyrrolate do not penetrate the CNS well and therefore are not useful in treating CNS diseases such as Parkinson disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: These drugs are muscarinic antagonists; the issue is CNS penetration, not a switch to nicotinic blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.C: They are antimuscarinics, not dopamine antagonists.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The brick attributes the difference to CNS penetration, not to pre-absorption metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: They block, rather than stimulate, muscarinic receptors.

questions.stem: A toxicologist is classifying several cholinesterase inhibitors by their mechanism of enzyme binding. Which of the following agents irreversibly phosphorylates the acetylcholinesterase enzyme?

explanation.summary: Organophosphates such as malathion irreversibly phosphorylate acetylcholinesterase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Malathion is an organophosphate, the class of irreversible indirect-acting cholinomimetics. These agents phosphorylate the AChE enzyme, and the phosphorylated complex can undergo aging that makes inhibition essentially permanent.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neostigmine is a reversible carbamate inhibitor that carbamoylates AChE, a process that can restore enzyme function.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Physostigmine is a reversible AChE inhibitor, not an organophosphate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pyridostigmine is a reversible inhibitor used in myasthenia gravis, not an irreversible one.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Edrophonium is a very short-acting reversible inhibitor used to diagnose myasthenia gravis.

questions.stem: A patient with severe anticholinergic toxicity is developing central symptoms including delirium and agitation. Which of the following cholinesterase inhibitors is able to cross the blood-brain barrier to reverse these central effects?

explanation.summary: Physostigmine is a tertiary amine that crosses the blood-brain barrier.
explanation.whyCorrect: Physostigmine is a tertiary (3 degree) amine, so it crosses the blood-brain barrier and can reverse both peripheral and central symptoms of anticholinergic toxicity, which is why it is the antidote for atropine overdose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neostigmine is a quaternary amine and cannot cross the blood-brain barrier.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pyridostigmine likewise does not cross the blood-brain barrier.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Edrophonium is used to diagnose myasthenia gravis and does not cross the blood-brain barrier.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Malathion is a toxic organophosphate insecticide, not a therapeutic antidote for anticholinergic toxicity.

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is asked to name a drug that raises synaptic acetylcholine by reversibly inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, rather than by binding acetylcholine receptors directly. Which of the following is the best example?

explanation.summary: Neostigmine is an indirect-acting reversible AChE inhibitor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Indirect-acting cholinomimetics inhibit the breakdown of acetylcholine by cholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft. Neostigmine is a reversible carbamate inhibitor and a classic example of this indirect mechanism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bethanechol is a direct-acting agent that binds acetylcholine receptors directly.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Carbachol is a direct-acting cholinomimetic, not an enzyme inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pilocarpine is a direct-acting agonist at muscarinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sarin inhibits acetylcholinesterase but does so irreversibly, not reversibly.

questions.stem: A farm worker is brought to the emergency department after heavy exposure to an organophosphate insecticide. The physician administers pralidoxime (2-PAM). Which of the following best describes the primary function of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It inhibits acetylcholine release from the nerve terminal

choices.label: C
choices.text: It binds and blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors

choices.label: D
choices.text: It accelerates aging of the enzyme-inhibitor complex

choices.label: E
choices.text: It directly blocks nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction

explanation.summary: Pralidoxime is an oxime reactivator that regenerates acetylcholinesterase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pralidoxime binds a separate site on AChE and, acting as a strong nucleophile, can displace the organophosphate and break the phosphorus-enzyme bond, regenerating normal cholinesterase function if given before aging occurs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pralidoxime does not affect acetylcholine release; it restores enzyme function.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blocking muscarinic receptors is the action of atropine, not pralidoxime.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pralidoxime prevents aging rather than accelerating it; aging makes reactivation impossible.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pralidoxime does not block nicotinic receptors.

questions.stem: A patient is diagnosed with physostigmine overdose and develops signs of excessive cholinergic stimulation. Which of the following is the most appropriate reversal agent?

explanation.summary: Atropine, an antimuscarinic agent, reverses excess cholinergic stimulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atropine is a competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. By blocking these receptors it counteracts the muscarinic effects of excess acetylcholine, making it the reversal agent for a reversible cholinesterase inhibitor overdose.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Donepezil is itself a cholinesterase inhibitor and would worsen the toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Malathion is a toxic organophosphate that would add to cholinergic excess.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pilocarpine is a direct muscarinic agonist and would intensify, not reverse, the symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neostigmine is another cholinesterase inhibitor and would aggravate the overdose.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man accidentally receives an overdose of neostigmine. On examination, which of the following ocular findings is most likely?

explanation.summary: Cholinesterase inhibitor excess causes miosis (pupillary constriction).
explanation.whyCorrect: Neostigmine increases acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, and stimulation of these receptors in the eye causes constriction of the pupillary sphincter muscle, producing miosis and increased lacrimation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mydriasis (pupil dilation) results from antimuscarinic or sympathetic activity, the opposite of excess acetylcholine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cycloplegia results from blocking muscarinic tone, not from cholinergic excess.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cholinergic stimulation increases, rather than decreases, lacrimation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A dilated pupil is an antimuscarinic effect and is not expected with cholinergic excess.

questions.stem: A neurologist suspects myasthenia gravis in a 32-year-old woman with fluctuating ptosis and diplopia. A very short-acting cholinesterase inhibitor is given, and her symptoms briefly improve, confirming the diagnosis. Which of the following agents was most likely used?

explanation.summary: Edrophonium is a short-acting inhibitor used to confirm myasthenia gravis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Edrophonium is a short-acting drug lasting 5 to 15 minutes that briefly improves the symptoms of myasthenia gravis, where acetylcholine stimulation is lacking at end organs, thereby confirming the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Physostigmine is used to treat atropine overdose, not to diagnose myasthenia gravis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Donepezil is used symptomatically in Alzheimer and Parkinson disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rivastigmine is used in dementia, not for diagnosing myasthenia gravis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyridostigmine has a longer duration and is used to treat, rather than diagnose, myasthenia gravis; neostigmine is used for glaucoma.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child ingests a plant containing atropine and develops dry, flushed skin, delirium, and tachycardia. Physostigmine is chosen as the antidote. Which property of physostigmine makes it especially suitable for treating this anticholinergic toxicity?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a quaternary amine confined to the periphery

choices.label: C
choices.text: It crosses the blood-brain barrier to treat central symptoms

explanation.summary: Physostigmine treats anticholinergic toxicity because it crosses the blood-brain barrier.
explanation.whyCorrect: Physostigmine is a reversible cholinesterase inhibitor that replenishes acetylcholine in the synapse. Because it crosses the blood-brain barrier, it is effective in treating both the peripheral and central symptoms of anticholinergic toxicity such as delirium and agitation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Physostigmine is a reversible inhibitor, not an irreversible phosphorylating organophosphate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Physostigmine is a tertiary amine; quaternary neostigmine is confined to the periphery.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Physostigmine raises acetylcholine by inhibiting its breakdown, not by blocking muscarinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reactivating acetylcholinesterase is the role of pralidoxime, not physostigmine.

questions.stem: A soldier is exposed to the nerve agent sarin, and the phosphorylated acetylcholinesterase complex undergoes a process that strengthens the phosphorus-enzyme bond, making it stable and resistant to reactivation. Which of the following best describes this process, and its clinical implication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carbamoylation, which allows spontaneous enzyme recovery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aging, after which oxime reactivators can no longer break the bond

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hydrolysis, which restores normal enzyme function

choices.label: D
choices.text: Competitive inhibition, which is easily reversed by acetylcholine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Allosteric activation, which increases enzyme turnover

explanation.summary: Aging strengthens the phosphorus-enzyme bond and prevents reactivation.
explanation.whyCorrect: After the organophosphate binds, aging strengthens the phosphorus-enzyme bond. Once aging has occurred the enzyme-inhibitor complex is more stable and difficult to break even with oxime reactivators, so pralidoxime must be given before aging. For sarin, aging occurs within about 10 minutes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carbamoylation describes reversible carbamate inhibitors, not the aging of organophosphates.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hydrolysis in this setting contributes to aging, which strengthens rather than restores the enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Organophosphate inhibition is essentially irreversible, not easily reversed competitive inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aging inactivates the enzyme; it does not allosterically activate it.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man who sprays pesticides on his farm presents with breathlessness, excessive sweating, drooling, diarrhea, pinpoint pupils, and a heart rate of 46 beats per minute. Which of the following best explains this constellation of findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blockade of muscarinic receptors by an antimuscarinic toxin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Depletion of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Histamine release from mast cell degranulation

explanation.summary: Organophosphate poisoning causes cholinergic excess, summarized by DUMBBELLSS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Exposure to organophosphate pesticides inhibits acetylcholinesterase, so acetylcholine accumulates and overstimulates muscarinic receptors. This produces the DUMBBELLSS toxidrome (diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchoconstriction, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation, sweating), matching this patient's findings.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antimuscarinic toxicity causes dry skin, dilated pupils, and tachycardia, the opposite of this picture.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Symptoms are due to too much, not too little, acetylcholine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-adrenergic overstimulation would cause tachycardia, not bradycardia and miosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Histamine release does not produce miosis, bradycardia, and the cholinergic secretory pattern seen here.

questions.stem: In managing severe organophosphate poisoning, atropine is administered together with pralidoxime. Which of the following best explains why atropine alone cannot fully treat this poisoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atropine agonizes muscarinic receptors and worsens symptoms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atropine cannot restore the function of the inhibited acetylcholinesterase enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atropine reactivates the enzyme but not before aging occurs

explanation.summary: Atropine blocks muscarinic effects but cannot regenerate the inhibited enzyme.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atropine is a competitive muscarinic antagonist that reverses muscarinic symptoms, but it cannot restore the function of the phosphorylated AChE enzyme. Because irreversible inhibition persists, atropine cannot be the only agent and is paired with pralidoxime, which regenerates the enzyme.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atropine antagonizes, rather than agonizes, muscarinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reactivating the enzyme is pralidoxime's role, not atropine's.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atropine blocks muscarinic, not nicotinic, receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine does not bind acetylcholinesterase; it blocks muscarinic receptors.

questions.stem: A patient with open-angle glaucoma is treated with neostigmine. Which of the following best describes how this drug lowers intraocular pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reduces resistance to aqueous humor outflow

choices.label: B
choices.text: It decreases production of aqueous humor by dilating the pupil

choices.label: C
choices.text: It blocks muscarinic receptors in the ciliary body

choices.label: E
choices.text: It causes mydriasis that opens the drainage angle

explanation.summary: Neostigmine manages glaucoma by reducing resistance to aqueous humor outflow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neostigmine is used to manage glaucoma by reducing resistance to aqueous humor outflow, a mechanism similar to that of the direct-acting agents carbachol and pilocarpine, thereby lowering intraocular pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: It works by improving outflow, and cholinergic stimulation causes miosis, not pupil dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neostigmine increases cholinergic activity by inhibiting AChE; it does not block muscarinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carbonic anhydrase inhibition is a different drug class and is not neostigmine's mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neostigmine causes miosis, not mydriasis; its benefit is reduced outflow resistance.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is found unresponsive and pulseless. EMS begins high-quality CPR and administers epinephrine intravenously during the resuscitation. Which of the following best explains the pharmacologic rationale for giving epinephrine during cardiac arrest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It selectively activates beta-2 receptors to produce bronchodilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: It activates both alpha and beta adrenergic receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: It blocks muscarinic M2 receptors to increase heart rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: It activates dopaminergic D1 receptors to dilate renal vascular beds

choices.label: E
choices.text: It activates alpha-1 receptors only, with no effect on the myocardium

explanation.summary: Epinephrine has roughly equal affinity for alpha and beta receptors, which is why it is the cornerstone drug in cardiac arrest.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epinephrine stimulates alpha-1 receptors to cause vasoconstriction, increasing systemic vascular resistance and improving perfusion pressure to the heart and brain. It simultaneously stimulates beta-1 receptors to increase heart rate and myocardial contractility, helping to restore effective circulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-2 bronchodilation is one action of epinephrine but is not the rationale for its use in arrest, and epinephrine is not beta-2 selective.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Epinephrine is an adrenergic agonist, not a muscarinic antagonist; it does not block M2 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dopaminergic D1 renal vasodilation is a property of dopamine, not epinephrine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Epinephrine acts on both alpha and beta receptors and has prominent beta-1 mediated cardiac effects.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with septic shock remains hypotensive despite fluids. She is started on a vasopressor that constricts all vascular beds and raises both systolic and diastolic blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance, with only weak beta activity. Which receptor does this agent target most strongly?

explanation.summary: Norepinephrine has greater affinity for alpha-1 than alpha-2 or beta receptors, producing widespread vasoconstriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: At therapeutic doses norepinephrine acts predominantly on alpha-1 receptors (alpha-1 much greater than alpha-2), constricting all vascular beds. This raises systolic and diastolic blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance, which is why it is used as a vasopressor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine has more alpha-1 than alpha-2 effect; alpha-2 is not the primary target driving vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-1 activation would increase contractility and rate, but the described effect is vascular tone; norepinephrine is not primarily beta.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-2 activation causes vasodilation and bronchodilation, the opposite of the vasoconstriction described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: M2 is a cholinergic receptor that slows the heart and is unrelated to adrenergic vasoconstriction.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with acute decompensated heart failure has a low cardiac output but adequate blood pressure. The team wants an agent that predominantly increases myocardial contractility through beta-1 receptors (with high beta-1 relative to beta-2 selectivity) and minimal pressor effect. Which agent best fits this profile?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is a beta-1 selective positive inotropic agent used to raise contractility.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dobutamine has higher affinity for beta-1 than beta-2 receptors. This beta-1 mediated effect increases cardiac contractility (positive inotropy) and heart rate, raising stroke volume and cardiac output, so it is classified as an inotrope rather than a vasopressor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine acts mainly on alpha-1 to raise vascular resistance and is used as a vasopressor, not a selective beta-1 inotrope.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dopamine activates beta-1 but also dopaminergic and (at higher doses) alpha-1 receptors, and is classified as a vasopressor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Isoproterenol activates beta-1 and beta-2 equally and is non-selective, not beta-1 selective.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Epinephrine acts on both alpha and beta receptors, giving it prominent vasopressor activity rather than selective inotropy.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A sympathomimetic is given at therapeutic doses to a critically ill patient. In addition to activating beta-1 receptors to increase cardiac contractility, it activates D1 and D2 dopaminergic receptors in the renal vascular beds to produce vasodilation. Which agent is being described?

explanation.summary: Dopamine uniquely activates dopaminergic D1 and D2 receptors in addition to adrenergic receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: At therapeutic doses dopamine activates beta-1 receptors to increase contractility, and higher doses increase alpha-1 effects. It also activates D1 and D2 dopaminergic receptors located in the renal vascular beds, producing renal vasodilation, a property unique to dopamine among these agents.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Dobutamine acts on beta-1 receptors and does not activate dopaminergic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Norepinephrine acts predominantly on alpha-1 and has no dopaminergic action.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Isoproterenol is a non-selective beta agonist without dopaminergic activity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Epinephrine acts on alpha and beta receptors but not dopaminergic receptors.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A drug is described as a potent activator of both beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors (beta-1 equals beta-2) with no alpha activity. It increases heart rate and contractility and also causes bronchodilation by relaxing bronchial smooth muscle. Which agent is this?

explanation.summary: Isoproterenol is a non-selective beta-1 and beta-2 agonist.
explanation.whyCorrect: Isoproterenol activates beta-1 and beta-2 receptors equally. Beta-1 activation increases heart rate and contractility (though less than dobutamine), and beta-2 activation causes bronchodilation by relaxing bronchial smooth muscle. It has no alpha activity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine is predominantly an alpha-1 agonist and is non-selective for beta, not an equal beta-1/beta-2 activator.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dobutamine is beta-1 selective (beta-1 greater than beta-2), not an equal beta-1/beta-2 agonist.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dopamine acts on beta-1, dopaminergic, and alpha-1 receptors rather than being a pure non-selective beta agonist.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylephrine is a selective alpha-1 agonist and would not cause bronchodilation or tachycardia.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Norepinephrine binds alpha-1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle, causing vasoconstriction. Which intracellular signaling change is produced by activation of this G-protein-coupled receptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased cAMP through a Gs pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased cAMP through a Gi pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased IP3, DAG and intracellular Ca2+ through a Gq pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct opening of a ligand-gated sodium channel

explanation.summary: Alpha-1 receptors are Gq-coupled and signal through the phospholipase C / IP3 / Ca2+ cascade.
explanation.whyCorrect: All alpha-1 receptor subtypes are Gq-type GPCRs. Gq activates phospholipase C, which splits PIP2 into IP3 and DAG and raises intracellular Ca2+. The rise in Ca2+ drives smooth muscle contraction and vasoconstriction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased cAMP via Gs is the mechanism of beta receptors, not alpha-1.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased cAMP via Gi is the mechanism of alpha-2 and M2 receptors, not alpha-1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ligand-gated ion channel opening describes nicotinic receptors, not GPCR-linked alpha-1 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The cGMP pathway is not the second messenger system used by alpha-1 adrenergic receptors.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Epinephrine increases heart rate and contractility by activating beta-1 receptors in the myocardium. Which G protein and second messenger mediate this effect?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gq, with increased IP3 and DAG

choices.label: D
choices.text: A ligand-gated cation channel with direct depolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gq, with increased intracellular Ca2+ only

explanation.summary: All beta receptors are Gs-coupled and act by raising cAMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-1 receptors are Gs-type GPCRs. Gs stimulates adenylyl cyclase, increasing cAMP and activating protein kinase A, which enhances cardiac contractility and heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Gi with decreased cAMP is the pathway of alpha-2 and M2 receptors, which slow the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gq with IP3 and DAG is the pathway of alpha-1 and M1/M3/M5 receptors, not beta-1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ligand-gated cation channels describe nicotinic receptors, not beta-1 GPCRs.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-1 signaling works through Gs and cAMP, not a Gq/Ca2+ mechanism.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Increased parasympathetic (cholinergic) tone slows the heart. Activation of which receptor is responsible for decreasing heart rate?

explanation.summary: Cholinergic slowing of the heart is mediated by the Gi-coupled M2 muscarinic receptor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholinergic activation decreases heart rate by activating M2 receptors, which are Gi-coupled GPCRs important in the heart. This opposes the sympathetic beta-1 effect in the constant tug-of-war between the two systems.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-1 activation increases, rather than decreases, heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha-1 receptors mediate vasoconstriction, not slowing of the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: M3 receptors act on glandular and smooth muscle (for example bronchial contraction), not the pacemaker slowing described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated channels at ganglia and muscle, not the cardiac rate-slowing receptor.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is asked to identify a physiologic response that results from cholinergic (parasympathetic) activation. Which of the following is the best example?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contraction of the iris causing pupillary constriction

explanation.summary: Cholinergic activation produces rest-and-digest effects such as pupillary constriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholinergic (parasympathetic) activation contracts the iris, producing pin-point pupils (miosis). This is a classic rest-and-digest response mediated by muscarinic receptors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased contractility is a sympathetic (beta-1) effect, not cholinergic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cholinergic activation causes bronchoconstriction; bronchodilation is a sympathetic beta-2 effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cholinergic activation increases gastric acid secretion; a decrease is not a parasympathetic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vasoconstriction is a sympathetic alpha-1 effect, not a cholinergic response.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Unlike muscarinic and adrenergic receptors, which are G-protein-coupled receptors, nicotinic acetylcholine receptors transmit their signal by a different mechanism. Which of the following best describes the nicotinic receptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A ligand-gated cation channel activated directly by acetylcholine

choices.label: B
choices.text: A Gs-coupled receptor that raises cAMP

choices.label: C
choices.text: A Gq-coupled receptor that raises intracellular Ca2+ via IP3

choices.label: D
choices.text: A Gi-coupled receptor that lowers cAMP

choices.label: E
choices.text: A voltage-gated calcium channel independent of acetylcholine

explanation.summary: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels, not GPCRs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated channels that are opened directly by acetylcholine, allowing cation influx and direct depolarization without using a G protein or second messenger.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Gs-coupled cAMP signaling describes beta adrenergic receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gq-coupled IP3/Ca2+ signaling describes alpha-1 and certain muscarinic receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gi-coupled lowering of cAMP describes alpha-2 and M2 receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nicotinic channels are ligand-gated (opened by ACh), not voltage-gated calcium channels.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A hemodynamically unstable patient needs support to restore contractility. The team selects an agent that increases cardiac contractility and cardiac output and is classified as a positive inotropic agent (inotrope) rather than a vasopressor. Which of the following agents fits this classification?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is classified as a positive inotropic agent; epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine are classified as vasopressors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dobutamine increases contractility (inotropy) and cardiac output through its beta-1 effect, and in practice is classified as an inotrope or positive inotropic agent used to restore hemodynamic stability.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epinephrine has potent vascular effects and is classified as a vasopressor.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine acts mainly on the vasculature and is classified as a vasopressor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dopamine has potent effects on the vasculature and is classified as a vasopressor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetylcholine is the endogenous cholinergic neurotransmitter, not a vasopressor or inotrope used in this setting.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: The term inotropy refers to a change in cardiac contractility. In a positive inotropic response, an increase in the intracellular concentration of a specific ion binds calmodulin and increases the force of cardiac muscle contraction. Which ion is responsible for this effect?

explanation.summary: Inotropy depends on intracellular calcium, which binds calmodulin to increase contraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ion of inotropy is Ca2+. When intracellular Ca2+ increases, it binds calmodulin, which increases muscle contraction; in cardiac cells this raises the force of contractility, defining a positive inotropic effect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sodium influx contributes to depolarization but is not the ion that binds calmodulin to drive contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Potassium governs repolarization and resting potential, not the calmodulin-mediated increase in contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chloride is not the ion described as binding calmodulin to increase contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Magnesium is not the ion responsible for the inotropic increase in cardiac contraction.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is found unresponsive with barely detectable pulse. EMS begins CPR and administers epinephrine. After a second dose, a rhythm returns with a blood pressure of 50/30 mm Hg. The rise in systemic vascular resistance produced by epinephrine during resuscitation is mediated primarily by activation of which of the following receptors?

explanation.summary: Epinephrine raises systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure through alpha1-mediated vasoconstriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: At therapeutic doses epinephrine activates both alpha and beta receptors. Its alpha1-adrenergic stimulation constricts vascular beds, increasing systemic vascular resistance and systolic blood pressure, which improves perfusion of vital organs such as the heart and brain during cardiac arrest.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta1 activation increases contractility and heart rate but does not raise systemic vascular resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta2 activation causes vasodilation and bronchodilation, which would tend to lower resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: M2 muscarinic receptors slow the heart and are not stimulated by epinephrine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D1 dopaminergic receptors produce renal vasodilation, not systemic vasoconstriction.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with anaphylaxis develops wheezing and difficulty breathing. She is given intramuscular epinephrine, and her bronchospasm rapidly improves. The bronchodilation produced by epinephrine is mediated by activation of which of the following receptors?

explanation.summary: Beta2 receptor activation relaxes bronchial smooth muscle, producing bronchodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epinephrine activates beta2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, causing relaxation and bronchodilation. This is why epinephrine is useful in the lungs during airway compromise.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha1 activation causes vasoconstriction, not airway relaxation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha2 activation reduces presynaptic norepinephrine release and does not open airways.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta1 activation increases cardiac contractility and rate rather than dilating bronchi.
whyWrongByChoice.E: M3 muscarinic (cholinergic) activation causes bronchoconstriction, the opposite effect.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man in septic shock remains hypotensive despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Norepinephrine is started, and his blood pressure improves. Which of the following best explains the primary mechanism by which norepinephrine raises this patient's blood pressure at therapeutic dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta2-mediated vasodilation of skeletal muscle beds

explanation.summary: Norepinephrine acts mainly on alpha1 receptors, constricting vessels and raising systemic vascular resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: At therapeutic dose norepinephrine has far greater affinity for alpha1 than alpha2 or beta receptors (alpha1 >> alpha2). It constricts essentially all vascular beds, raising both systolic and diastolic blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Norepinephrine is non-selective and weak at beta2; it does not predominantly cause vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Renal dopaminergic vasodilation is a feature of dopamine, not norepinephrine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Norepinephrine does not activate muscarinic receptors or slow the heart as its pressor mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchodilation is a beta2 effect and is not the mechanism raising blood pressure.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with decompensated heart failure has low cardiac output but adequate blood pressure. She is started on dobutamine to improve myocardial contractility. Dobutamine produces its positive inotropic effect primarily through which of the following receptors?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is a beta1-selective agonist that increases cardiac contractility.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dobutamine has higher affinity for beta1 than beta2 receptors. Beta1 activation increases cardiac contractility (positive inotropy) and heart rate (positive chronotropy), raising stroke volume and cardiac output, which is why it is classified as an inotrope.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dobutamine has little alpha1 activity and does not act mainly as a vasoconstrictor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta2 receptors are found mostly in the lungs and mediate bronchodilation, not inotropy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: M2 muscarinic activation slows the heart and decreases contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D1 dopaminergic receptors mediate renal vasodilation, not myocardial contractility.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A patient in shock is given dopamine. In addition to its cardiac effects, dopamine causes vasodilation of the renal vascular beds. This renal vasodilation is mediated by activation of which of the following receptors?

explanation.summary: Dopamine dilates renal vessels by activating dopaminergic (D1/D2) receptors in the renal vascular beds.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dopamine activates D1 and D2 dopaminergic receptors located in the renal vascular beds, producing vasodilation there. At therapeutic doses it also activates beta1 to increase contractility, with alpha1 effects appearing at higher doses.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha1 activation causes vasoconstriction, the opposite of renal vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta1 activation increases cardiac contractility but does not mediate renal vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta2 receptors are largely in the lungs and skeletal muscle vasculature, not the mechanism described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: M3 muscarinic receptors are cholinergic and are not the target that produces dopamine's renal effect.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Activation of an alpha1 adrenergic receptor on vascular smooth muscle increases intracellular calcium and causes contraction. Through which of the following second-messenger mechanisms does this receptor act?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stimulation of adenylyl cyclase to raise cAMP

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibition of adenylyl cyclase to lower cAMP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of phospholipase C generating IP3 and DAG

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opening of a ligand-gated sodium channel

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct activation of guanylyl cyclase to raise cGMP

explanation.summary: Alpha1 receptors are Gq-coupled and signal through phospholipase C, IP3/DAG, and calcium.
explanation.whyCorrect: All alpha1 receptor subtypes are Gq-type GPCRs. The Gq pathway activates phospholipase C, which generates IP3 and DAG and triggers calcium release, driving calcium-dependent smooth muscle contraction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Raising cAMP via adenylyl cyclase is the Gs pathway used by beta receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lowering cAMP is the Gi pathway used by alpha2 and M2 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ligand-gated sodium channels describe nicotinic receptors, not alpha1 GPCRs.
whyWrongByChoice.E: cGMP/guanylyl cyclase signaling is not the mechanism of alpha1 receptors.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Increased vagal (parasympathetic) tone slows the heart rate through muscarinic M2 receptors in the myocardium. These M2 receptors are coupled to which of the following G proteins?

explanation.summary: M2 muscarinic receptors are Gi-coupled and lower cAMP, contributing to slowing of the heart.
explanation.whyCorrect: Among the muscarinic receptors, M2 (and M4) couple to Gi, which inhibits adenylyl cyclase and lowers cAMP. M2 receptors in the heart mediate the parasympathetic decrease in heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No muscarinic receptor is a Gs-type GPCR; Gs is used by beta receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gq coupling applies to M1, M3, and M5, not M2.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ligand-gated cation channels describe nicotinic, not muscarinic, receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Muscarinic receptors are GPCRs, not tyrosine kinase receptors.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A drug is described as a potent activator of both beta1 and beta2 adrenergic receptors with essentially no alpha activity. It increases heart rate and contractility and also causes bronchodilation. Which of the following agents is being described?

explanation.summary: Isoproterenol is a non-selective beta agonist (beta1 = beta2).
explanation.whyCorrect: Isoproterenol potently activates both beta1 and beta2 receptors (beta1 = beta2). It increases heart rate and contractility and produces bronchodilation via beta2, with no significant alpha activity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Norepinephrine acts mainly on alpha1 (alpha1 >> alpha2) and constricts vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dopamine acts on beta1, alpha1 at higher doses, and dopaminergic renal receptors, not selectively on both beta subtypes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dobutamine is beta1-selective (beta1 > beta2) and is used mainly for inotropy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetylcholine is a cholinergic (parasympathetic) transmitter, not a beta agonist.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: A 40-year-old farm worker presents after organophosphate exposure with excessive cholinergic activity. Which of the following findings is most consistent with cholinergic (muscarinic) activation?

explanation.summary: Cholinergic activation contracts the iris (miosis) and promotes rest-and-digest effects.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholinergic activation causes the iris to contract, producing pin-point pupils (miosis). It reflects parasympathetic rest-and-digest activity, which also slows the heart, constricts bronchi, and increases secretions.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cholinergic activation decreases heart rate through M2 receptors, not increases it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cholinergic activation causes bronchoconstriction, not bronchodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cholinergic activation increases gastric acid production rather than decreasing it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vasoconstriction and raised blood pressure are adrenergic (sympathetic) effects.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: In clinical practice, sympathomimetic agents are classified as either vasopressors or positive inotropes based on their dominant hemodynamic action. Which of the following agents is classified primarily as a positive inotrope rather than a vasopressor?

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is classified as a positive inotrope, whereas epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine are vasopressors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dobutamine mainly increases contractility (inotropy) and cardiac output through beta1 activation, so it is classified as a positive inotrope. Epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine have potent vascular effects and are typically classified as vasopressors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epinephrine is classified in practice as a vasopressor due to its potent vascular effects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Norepinephrine is a prototypical vasopressor that raises systemic vascular resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dopamine is typically classified as a vasopressor with additional renal dopaminergic effects.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetylcholine is a parasympathetic transmitter and is neither a vasopressor nor an inotrope.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: Inotropic agents increase the force of cardiac contraction. According to the mechanism of inotropy, the intracellular ion that binds calmodulin to increase cardiac muscle contraction is which of the following?

explanation.summary: The ion of inotropy is calcium, which binds calmodulin to drive muscle contraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inotropy refers to calcium. When intracellular calcium rises, it binds calmodulin and increases muscle contraction; in cardiac cells this increases the force of contraction, the basis of positive inotropic agents.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sodium influx contributes to depolarization but is not the ion that binds calmodulin to drive inotropy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Potassium governs repolarization and resting potential, not the calmodulin-mediated contraction step.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chloride is not the second messenger that mediates increased cardiac contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Magnesium is not the ion described as the mediator of inotropy in this mechanism.

questions.topicName: Autonomic Nervous System - Vasopressors and Inotropes

questions.stem: At the autonomic ganglia of both the sympathetic and parasympathetic systems, acetylcholine transmits the signal by acting on nicotinic receptors. Compared with the other autonomic receptors, nicotinic receptors are distinct because they are which of the following?

explanation.summary: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels activated by acetylcholine, unlike the GPCR autonomic receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nicotinic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels opened by acetylcholine, allowing cation influx and depolarization. This distinguishes them from the adrenergic and muscarinic receptors, which are all GPCRs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gs-coupled GPCRs describe beta receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gi-coupled GPCRs describe alpha2 and M2/M4 receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gq-coupled GPCRs describe alpha1 and M1/M3/M5 receptors, not nicotinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autonomic receptors are not tyrosine kinase receptors.
